feynman.0: Here is the curious thing: a magnetic field will push on a moving charge — but only on the part of the motion that cuts across the field. Move along the field and it does nothing at all. So in this topic we’ll work out the force law, first for a lone charge and then for a current, watch the circular paths that come out of it, and pick up the little notation you need for thinking in three dimensions.
feynman.1: Park a charge in a magnetic field and let it sit still, and nothing happens — no force at all. The charge has to be moving, and even then, only the part of its motion that crosses the field gets to count. Two cases cover everything you’ll meet in this course: the velocity straight across the field, and the velocity straight along it.
feynman.2: We measure the field strength B in tesla (T). The formula is for when the velocity is perpendicular to the field. Send the charge moving parallel — or antiparallel — to the field instead, and the force is exactly zero, nothing. Fire a proton straight along a field line and it just sails on in a straight line at constant speed, as if the field weren’t there.
feynman.3: Trap No crossing, no force — remember that one. A charge moving along the field direction feels zero magnetic force, no matter how fast it goes or how strong you make the field. Same story for a wire whose current runs along the field (see Force on a current-carrying wire). The usual slip is to reach for F = qvB before stopping to check the angle between v and B.
feynman.4: To get the direction, use the right-hand slap rule, drawn in FIG 4.1. Hold your right hand flat, thumb stuck out at right angles to the fingers: point the thumb along the velocity of a positive charge, the fingers along the field — and your palm ends up facing the way the force points. And the force always comes out perpendicular to both the velocity and the field.
feynman.5: For a negative charge, just turn the answer around. Either work the slap rule and flip the force, or aim your thumb against the electron’s motion — a negative charge heading left is the same as a positive charge heading right. You’ll do this little reversal in every electron-beam problem there is.
feynman.6: Since v, B and F are all at right angles to one another, one of the three will usually be pointing straight along your line of sight in a diagram. So we draw a field going into the page as a grid of × symbols and a field coming out of the page as • symbols; the whole convention is laid out in Into and out of the page.
feynman.7: Worked example. Lucia studies a beam in which each proton travels at 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ at right angles to a uniform 0.25 T magnetic field. The magnitude of the force the field puts on one proton is F = qvB = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 4.0 × 10⁶ × 0.25 = 1.6 × 10⁻¹³ N, pointing perpendicular to the velocity, the way the slap rule tells you.
feynman.8: Now here’s a force that never lets up in size and always points square across the velocity — and that is exactly the recipe for uniform circular motion, the one you met in Unit 3 AOS 1. So a charge crossing a uniform magnetic field has to travel in a circle: the magnetic force is doing the job of the centripetal force. Set qvB equal to mv²/r, shuffle it around, and out drops the radius.
feynman.9: Four things follow straight off. A faster particle sweeps out a wider circle (r ∝ v), and a stronger field bends it into a tighter one (r ∝ 1/B). A heavier particle, carrying more inertia, swings round on a wider circle (r ∝ m). And flip the sign of the charge and you flip the force, so electrons and protons that enter the same field with the same velocity go round in opposite directions.
feynman.10: The magnetic force does no work on the charge — none. To do work you need a force component pointing along the motion, and the magnetic force never has one: it sits perpendicular to the velocity at every instant. So the speed stays put, the kinetic energy stays put, and only the direction of travel changes, as FIG 4.2 shows. A magnetic field can steer a charged particle this way and that, but it can’t speed it up or slow it down — for that you need an electric field.
feynman.11: Key idea A uniform magnetic field changes which way a moving charge is going, never how fast. Speed, kinetic energy and radius all hold steady; the velocity just keeps turning.
feynman.12: Worked example. Anika fires an electron at 2.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ into a uniform 1.5 mT field, perpendicular to the field. Ignore relativistic effects. Convert the field first: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 2.0 × 10⁷) ÷ (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 1.5 × 10⁻³) = 7.6 × 10⁻² m; the electron loops round with a radius of 7.6 cm, at a speed that never budges.
feynman.13: Trap Millitesla. Field strengths get quoted in mT all the time, so convert before you substitute, like this: 2.5 mT = 2.5 × 10⁻³ T. Drop the raw number in and you make the force 1000 times too big and the radius 1000 times too small — and the wrong answer comes out looking perfectly reasonable, which is what gets you.
feynman.14: Connects The Australian Synchrotron’s storage ring is just r = mv/qB blown up to the size of a building: bending magnets hold the electrons on a closed circular path. Turning means accelerating, and Unit 4 will show that accelerating charges radiate — which is exactly why the ring works as a light source. The full accelerator pipeline gets put together in 8 · Motors & accelerators.
feynman.15: A current is nothing but charge on the move, so a wire carrying current across a magnetic field feels a force: the field pushes on every drifting charge, and the charges, with no way to escape the metal, hand the force over to the wire itself. For a straight conductor sitting at right angles to a uniform field:
feynman.16: n just counts how many conductors sit side by side in the field: for a coil of 20 turns, n = 20 and you get 20 times the force on a single wire. And only the length that’s actually inside the field counts toward l (mind the lowercase l, the way it’s written on the formula sheet).
feynman.17: The slap rule hands you the direction again, only now the thumb points along the conventional current: thumb along I, fingers along B, palm facing along F, as FIG 4.3 shows. And the zero-force case comes right back, in full: if the current runs parallel or antiparallel to the field, the force is zero. Lay a rectangular coil in a field and the two sides whose current crosses the field feel forces; the two sides whose current runs along the field feel nothing. That one fact is the whole secret of the DC motor coming up in the next topic.
feynman.18: Worked example. Magnus tests a flat coil of 20 turns. One side of the coil is 0.050 m long, carries a current of 1.5 A and lies at right angles to a uniform 0.30 T field. The magnitude of the force the field puts on that side is F = nIlB = 20 × 1.5 × 0.050 × 0.30 = 0.45 N. The opposite side carries the same current the other way, so it feels an equal force in the opposite direction — a pair of forces that can spin the coil round.
feynman.19: Connects This section is the motor principle in a nutshell: a magnetic field makes a current-carrying coil move. Unit 3 AOS 3 runs the whole thing backwards — move a coil through a field and you drive a current — and that reversal, electromagnetic induction through changing magnetic flux, is how every generator in the grid earns its keep.
feynman.20: Magnetic-force problems are three-dimensional through and through: v (or I), B and F are all at right angles to one another, so there’s no way to get all three flat on the page at once. The trick is to send one of them straight along your line of sight and picture it as an arrow: seen from behind, you spot the crossed feathers of the tail — ×, into the page; seen coming at you, the point — •, out of the page. Any diagram using the symbols ought to carry a key, something like "× = field into the page".
feynman.21: Here’s a routine that never lets you down on a direction question: first, read the field direction straight off the symbols. Second, point your flat right hand’s thumb along the velocity of a positive charge — turned around for an electron — or along the conventional current. Third, line the fingers up with the field; now your palm faces along the force.
feynman.22: Try one: an electron heads up the page through a field pointing out of the page (•). For a positive charge moving up the page, the slap rule gives a force to the right; but the electron’s negative charge flips that around, so the force the field puts on the electron points to the left of the page.

sagan.0: A magnetic field reaches out and pushes upon a moving charge — yet only upon the part of its motion that dares to cross the field. In this topic we follow that force where it leads: the law that governs free charges and the currents we send through wires, the great circles those forces trace, and the simple notation that lets us reason in all three dimensions of space at once.
sagan.1: Hold a charge perfectly still, and a magnetic field will do nothing to it whatever. Only a charge in motion feels the force — and even then, only that part of the motion which crosses the field is counted; the rest is ignored entirely. Two cases between them tell the whole story for us here: velocity that runs perpendicular to the field, and velocity that runs along it.
sagan.2: The strength of the field, B, we measure in tesla (T). The formula holds when the velocity cuts across the field at right angles. But let the charge travel parallel to the field — or directly against it — and the force vanishes to exactly nothing. A proton sent gliding along a field line simply continues, straight and unhurried, at a speed that never changes.
sagan.3: Trap Where there is no crossing, there is no force. A charge moving along the direction of the field feels nothing at all, however furiously it travels and however mighty the field. The same is true of a wire whose current runs along the field (see Force on a current-carrying wire). To reach for F = qvB before first asking how v and B are angled to one another is among the most common of all errors.
sagan.4: The direction of the force we read from the right-hand slap rule, drawn for us in FIG 4.1. Hold the right hand flat, the thumb set at right angles to the fingers: let the thumb follow the velocity of a positive charge, the fingers follow the field — and the palm then turns to face the very direction of the force. That force stands always perpendicular to both the velocity and the field, faithful to neither but bound to each.
sagan.5: For a negative charge, turn the answer around. Either apply the slap rule and reverse the force you find, or set the thumb against the electron's motion instead: a negative charge drifting left is, for all the field cares, a positive charge drifting right. This single reversal lies at the heart of every electron-beam problem you will ever meet.
sagan.6: Because v, B and F stand mutually perpendicular, one of the three will almost always point straight along your line of sight in a diagram, diving into the page or rising out of it. A field into the page we draw as a grid of × symbols, a field out of the page as a scattering of • symbols; the whole convention is laid out in Into and out of the page.
sagan.7: Worked example. Lucia studies a beam in which each proton travels at 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ at right angles to a uniform 0.25 T magnetic field. The magnitude of the force the field exerts on a single proton is F = qvB = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 4.0 × 10⁶ × 0.25 = 1.6 × 10⁻¹³ N, directed perpendicular to the velocity, just as the slap rule promises.
sagan.8: A force of unchanging magnitude that holds itself forever perpendicular to the velocity — this is precisely the condition for uniform circular motion, the same we met in Unit 3 AOS 1. And so a charge sweeping across a uniform magnetic field must trace a perfect circle: the magnetic force becomes the centripetal force that bends it inward. Set qvB equal to mv²/r, rearrange, and the radius of that circle falls out into our hands.
sagan.9: Four consequences follow at once, each as plain as the last. A swifter particle sweeps a wider circle (r ∝ v), while a stronger field bends a tighter one (r ∝ 1/B). A heavier particle, carrying more inertia, turns on a wider circle still (r ∝ m). And reverse the sign of the charge and you reverse the force itself, so that electrons and protons entering the same field with the same velocity wheel away in opposite senses, mirror twins parting company.
sagan.10: Upon the charge the magnetic force does no work at all. Work demands a force with some component along the motion, and the magnetic force never offers one: at every instant it stands square across the velocity. So the speed holds constant and the kinetic energy holds constant; only the direction of the motion turns, as shown in FIG 4.2. A magnetic field may steer a charged particle wherever it pleases, yet it can never quicken or slow it — any change of speed must come instead from an electric field.
sagan.11: Key idea A uniform magnetic field turns the direction of a moving charge and never its speed. Speed, kinetic energy and radius all remain constant; the velocity alone wheels around.
sagan.12: Worked example. Anika fires an electron at 2.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ into a uniform 1.5 mT field, perpendicular to the field. Ignore relativistic effects. Convert the field first: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 2.0 × 10⁷) ÷ (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 1.5 × 10⁻³) = 7.6 × 10⁻² m; the electron circles forever on a radius of 7.6 cm, at a speed that never alters.
sagan.13: Trap The millitesla. Field strengths come quoted, time and again, in mT: convert before you substitute, as in 2.5 mT = 2.5 × 10⁻³ T. Feed in the raw number and the force swells a thousandfold too large while the radius shrinks a thousandfold too small — and the false answer, worst of all, wears a perfectly plausible face.
sagan.14: Connects The storage ring of the Australian Synchrotron is nothing other than r = mv/qB raised to the scale of a building: its bending magnets hold electrons forever upon a closed circular path. To turn is to accelerate, and Unit 4 will reveal that accelerating charges pour out radiation — which is the whole reason the ring shines as a source of light. The full accelerator pipeline is assembled in 8 · Motors & accelerators.
sagan.15: A current is simply charge on the move, and so a wire bearing current across a magnetic field must feel a force: the field lays hold of every drifting charge within, and those charges, unable to escape the metal that imprisons them, hand the force on to the wire itself. For a straight conductor set at right angles to a uniform field:
sagan.16: n counts how many conductors lie side by side within the field: for a coil of 20 turns, n = 20, and the force grows to 20 times the force upon a single wire. Only the length that truly lies inside the field contributes to l (note the lowercase l, just as it stands on the formula sheet).
sagan.17: The slap rule gives the direction once more, the thumb now following the conventional current: thumb along I, fingers along B, palm facing along F, as shown in FIG 4.3. And here the zero-force case returns to us in full: if the current runs parallel or antiparallel to the field, the force is zero. In a rectangular coil lying within a field, the two sides whose current cuts across the field feel forces; the two sides whose current runs along the field feel nothing whatever. That single fact is the key that unlocks the DC motor in the next topic.
sagan.18: Worked example. Magnus tests a flat coil of 20 turns. One side of the coil is 0.050 m long, carries a current of 1.5 A and lies at right angles to a uniform 0.30 T field. The magnitude of the force the field exerts on that side is F = nIlB = 20 × 1.5 × 0.050 × 0.30 = 0.45 N. The opposite side carries the same current the opposite way, so it feels an equal force in the opposite direction — a pair of forces poised to rotate the whole coil.
sagan.19: Connects This section is the motor principle laid bare: a magnetic field sets a current-carrying coil into motion. Unit 3 AOS 3 runs the same machinery in reverse — move a coil through a field and a current springs up — and that reversal, electromagnetic induction born of changing magnetic flux, is the principle by which every generator in the grid does its work.
sagan.20: Magnetic-force problems live in all three dimensions: v (or I), B and F stand mutually perpendicular, so the three of them can never all lie flat upon the page together. The standard convention sends one of them off along the line of sight and pictures it as an arrow: seen from behind, the crossed fletching of the tail — ×, into the page; seen rushing toward you, the point — •, out of the page. Every diagram that wields these symbols should carry a key, for example "× = field into the page".
sagan.21: Here is a routine you may trust for any question of direction. First, read the direction of the field straight off the symbols. Second, set the flat right hand's thumb along the velocity of positive charge — reversed, of course, for an electron — or along the conventional current. Third, lay the fingers along the field; the palm now turns to face along the force.
sagan.22: Consider, for example, an electron travelling up the page through a field that points out of the page (•). For a positive charge moving up the page, the slap rule yields a force to the right; but the electron's negative charge reverses this, and so the force the field exerts upon the electron points to the left of the page.

tyson.0: A magnetic field reaches out and shoves a moving charge around — but only if that motion crosses the field. Stand still, feel nothing. Here’s the tour: the force law for free charges and for currents, the circular paths that fall out of it, and the shorthand for thinking in three dimensions.

Same deal for a wire whose current runs along the field (see Force on a current-carrying wire). Slapping F = qvB down without first checking the angle between v and B is one of the most common ways to go wrong.

Hold the right hand flat, thumb at right angles to the fingers: thumb along the velocity of a positive charge, fingers along the field — and the palm now faces the direction of the force. The force is always perpendicular to both the velocity and the field — no exceptions.

A magnetic field can steer a charged particle all day long, but it cannot speed it up or slow it down — any change of speed has to come from an electric field.

cox.0: A magnetic field reaches out and pushes on moving charge — but, and this is the lovely part, only on the motion that crosses the field. cox.1: Leave a charge sitting still in a magnetic field and nothing happens at all — no force, no push, nothing. It is only when a charge moves that the field takes hold of it, and even then only the part of the motion that crosses the field matters. Two cases cover everything we need in this course: velocity perpendicular to the field, and velocity parallel to it.
cox.2: The field strength B is measured in tesla (T). This formula is the one to reach for when the velocity runs perpendicular to the field. And when the charge moves parallel — or antiparallel — to the field, the force is exactly zero, nothing at all. Fire a proton straight along a field line and it simply sails on in a straight line, at constant speed, untouched.
cox.3: Trap No crossing, no force. A charge drifting along the field direction feels zero magnetic force — however fast it goes, however strong the field. Hold the right hand flat, thumb at right angles to the fingers: thumb along the velocity of a positive charge, fingers along the field — and the palm then faces the way the force pushes. The force always comes out perpendicular to both the velocity and the field, every single time.
cox.5: For a negative charge, simply turn the answer around. Either apply the slap rule and flip the force, or point the thumb against the electron's motion: a negative charge heading left is just a positive charge heading right. This little reversal turns up in every electron-beam problem you will ever meet.
cox.6: Because v, B and F are mutually perpendicular, one of the three will usually be pointing straight along your line of sight in a diagram. A field heading into the page we draw as a grid of × symbols, and a field coming out of the page as • symbols; the whole convention is set out in Into and out of the page.
cox.7: Worked example. Lucia studies a beam in which each proton travels at 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ at right angles to a uniform 0.25 T magnetic field. cox.8: A force that holds its magnitude steady while staying forever perpendicular to the velocity — that is precisely the recipe for uniform circular motion, which we met back in Unit 3 AOS 1. So a charge crossing a uniform magnetic field traces out a circle: the magnetic force is the centripetal force, quietly doing the whole job. Set qvB equal to mv²/r, rearrange, and the radius falls out.

The field pushes on every drifting charge, and those charges, stuck inside the metal with nowhere to go, hand that push straight to the wire. For a straight conductor at right angles to a uniform field:
nye.16: n just counts how many conductors sit side by side in the field: for a coil of 20 turns, n = 20, and the force is 20 times the force on a single wire — easy! And only the length that’s actually inside the field counts toward l (note the lowercase l, exactly as it appears on the formula sheet).
nye.17: The slap rule hands you the direction again, except now your thumb points along the conventional current: thumb along I, fingers along B, palm facing along F, as shown in FIG 4.3. And our zero-force case comes roaring right back: if the current runs parallel or antiparallel to the field, the force is zero. Picture a rectangular coil lying in a field — the two sides whose current crosses the field feel forces; the two sides whose current runs along the field feel nothing. Hang onto that one fact, because it’s the whole secret behind the DC motor in the next topic!
nye.18: Worked example. Magnus is testing a flat coil of 20 turns. One side of the coil is 0.050 m long, carries a current of 1.5 A and lies at right angles to a uniform 0.30 T field. Let’s find the force the field exerts on that side: F = nIlB = 20 × 1.5 × 0.050 × 0.30 = 0.45 N. Now the opposite side carries that same current the opposite way, so it feels an equal force in the opposite direction — a matched pair of forces that can spin the coil around!
nye.19: Connects This, right here, is the motor principle: a magnetic field makes a current-carrying coil move! And in Unit 3 AOS 3 we’ll run the whole thing backwards — move a coil through a field and you drive a current instead. That reversal, electromagnetic induction through changing magnetic flux, is exactly how every generator in the grid does its job.
nye.20: Here’s the catch with magnetic-force problems — they’re three-dimensional! v (or I), B and F are all mutually perpendicular, so there’s no way to fit all three flat on the page at once. So we use a clever trick: stick one of them along your line of sight and draw it as an arrow you’re looking down. Seen from behind, you spot the crossed fletching of the tail — ×, into the page; seen coming toward you, the sharp point — •, out of the page! Every diagram that uses these symbols should carry a key, for example "× = field into the page".
nye.21: Here’s a foolproof routine for any direction question. First, read the field direction straight off the symbols. Second, point your flat right hand’s thumb along the velocity of positive charge — flip it for an electron — or along the conventional current. Third, line your fingers up with the field, and boom: your palm now faces along the force!
nye.22: Let’s try one. Say an electron travels up the page through a field that points out of the page (•). For a positive charge heading up the page, the slap rule gives a force to the right — but the electron’s negative charge flips that around, so the force the field exerts on the electron points to the left of the page.

munroe.0: A magnetic field pushes on a moving charge, but only on the part of the motion that cuts across the field. Move along the field and it does nothing to you. This topic covers the force law for free charges and for currents, the circular paths that result, and the notation you need to draw any of it in three dimensions.
munroe.1: A charge sitting still feels no magnetic force at all. You have to be moving, and only the part of your motion that crosses the field actually counts. Two cases handle everything in this course: velocity straight across the field, and velocity straight along it.
munroe.2: The field strength B is measured in tesla (T). The formula works when the velocity is perpendicular to the field. Move the charge parallel to the field — or exactly backwards along it — and the force is not small, it is exactly zero. A proton fired straight down a field line just keeps going in a straight line at the same speed, as if the field weren't there.
munroe.3: Trap No crossing, no force. A charge moving along the field direction feels zero magnetic force, no matter how fast it goes and no matter how strong the field is. The same goes for a wire whose current runs along the field (see Force on a current-carrying wire). Reaching for F = qvB before you have checked the angle between v and B is how people get this wrong.
munroe.4: The direction of the force comes from the right-hand slap rule, drawn in FIG 4.1. Hold your right hand flat with the thumb stuck out at right angles to the fingers: thumb along the velocity of a positive charge, fingers along the field, and your palm now points the way the force points. The force is always at right angles to both the velocity and the field — it can't be anything else.
munroe.5: For a negative charge, you flip the answer. Either run the slap rule and reverse the force, or just point your thumb against the electron's motion: an electron moving left behaves exactly like a positive charge moving right. That flip shows up in every electron-beam problem, so it pays to do it on purpose rather than by accident.
munroe.6: Because v, B and F are all at right angles to each other, one of the three almost always points straight at you or straight away from you in a diagram. A field going into the page is drawn as a grid of × symbols and a field coming out of the page as • symbols; the convention is laid out in Into and out of the page.
munroe.7: Worked example. Lucia studies a beam in which each proton travels at 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ at right angles to a uniform 0.25 T magnetic field. The size of the force the field puts on one proton is F = qvB = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 4.0 × 10⁶ × 0.25 = 1.6 × 10⁻¹³ N, pointed at right angles to the velocity, the way the slap rule says.
munroe.8: A force that stays the same size and always points at right angles to the velocity is exactly the recipe for uniform circular motion, which you met in Unit 3 AOS 1. So a charge crossing a uniform magnetic field goes in a circle: the magnetic force is doing the job of the centripetal force. Set qvB equal to mv²/r, rearrange, and you have the radius.
munroe.9: Four things follow straight from that, and none of them are surprising once you look at them. A faster particle draws a bigger circle (r ∝ v), and a stronger field bends it into a tighter one (r ∝ 1/B). A heavier particle has more inertia, so it turns more reluctantly and sweeps a wider circle (r ∝ m). And flipping the sign of the charge flips the force, so an electron and a proton sent into the same field with the same velocity go around opposite ways.
munroe.10: The magnetic force does no work on the charge. Doing work needs a force component pointing along the motion, and the magnetic force never has one — it sits at right angles to the velocity at every single instant. So the speed doesn't change and the kinetic energy doesn't change; the only thing that changes is which way the particle is headed, as shown in FIG 4.2. A magnetic field can steer a charged particle but it can't speed it up or slow it down — for that you need an electric field.
munroe.11: Key idea A uniform magnetic field changes which way a moving charge is going, and that is all it changes. The speed, the kinetic energy and the radius all stay put; only the direction of the velocity turns.
munroe.12: Worked example. Anika fires an electron at 2.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ into a uniform 1.5 mT field, straight across the field. Ignore relativistic effects. Fix the units first: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 2.0 × 10⁷) ÷ (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 1.5 × 10⁻³) = 7.6 × 10⁻² m; the electron goes around in a circle of radius 7.6 cm at a speed that never changes.
munroe.13: Trap Millitesla. Field strengths are handed to you in mT all the time, so convert before you substitute, as in 2.5 mT = 2.5 × 10⁻³ T. Drop the raw number straight in and your force comes out 1000 times too big and your radius 1000 times too small — and the wrong answer looks perfectly reasonable, which is the dangerous part.
munroe.14: Connects The Australian Synchrotron's storage ring is just r = mv/qB blown up to the size of a building: bending magnets hold the electrons on a closed circular path. Turning is accelerating, and Unit 4 will show that accelerating charges give off radiation — which is the whole reason the ring works as a light source. The full accelerator pipeline gets put together in 8 · Motors & accelerators.

munroe.16: n is the count of how many conductors sit side by side in the field: for a coil of 20 turns, n = 20 and the force is 20 times what a single wire would feel. The zero-force case comes back here in full: if the current runs parallel or antiparallel to the field, the force is zero. In a rectangular coil lying in a field, the two sides whose current crosses the field get pushed; the two sides whose current runs along the field get nothing. The opposite side carries the same current the other way, so it feels a force of the same size pointing the opposite way — a pair of pushes that can spin the coil.

bronowski.0: A magnetic field reaches out and pushes upon a moving charge—but it is particular in what it touches, for it acts only on that part of the motion which cuts across the field. This topic follows the law of that force, first for a free charge and then for a current, traces the circles into which moving charges are bent, and sets down the small notation by which we picture a three-dimensional world on a flat page.
bronowski.1: Upon a charge that sits still a magnetic field lays no hand at all. Only a charge in motion feels the force, and even then only the portion of its motion that crosses the field is counted. Two cases between them hold everything this course will ask of you: a velocity set at right angles to the field, and a velocity running parallel to it.
bronowski.2: The strength of the field, B, we measure in tesla (T). The formula holds when the velocity stands perpendicular to the field. But let the charge move parallel to the field—or directly against it—and the force falls to exactly zero. A proton sent travelling along a field line keeps to a straight road, its speed unchanged.
bronowski.3: Trap Where there is no crossing, there is no force. A charge moving along the direction of the field feels no magnetic force whatever, no matter how fast it flies or how mighty the field. The same is true of a wire whose current runs along the field (see Force on a current-carrying wire). To reach for F = qvB without first asking what angle stands between v and B is among the commonest of mistakes.
bronowski.4: The direction of the force is read from the right-hand slap rule, as FIG 4.1 shows. Hold the right hand open and flat, the thumb set square to the fingers: the thumb along the velocity of a positive charge, the fingers along the field—and the palm then faces the way the force points. That force stands always perpendicular both to the velocity and to the field.
bronowski.5: For a negative charge, turn the answer about. Either work the slap rule and then reverse the force, or set the thumb against the electron's motion: a negative charge moving to the left is the same as a positive charge moving to the right. This single reversal belongs to every electron-beam problem you will meet.
bronowski.6: Because v, B and F stand mutually perpendicular, one of the three will commonly point straight along the line of sight in a diagram. A field running into the page we draw as a grid of × symbols, and a field coming out of the page as • symbols; the convention is laid out in Into and out of the page.
bronowski.7: Worked example. Lucia studies a beam in which each proton travels at 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ at right angles to a uniform 0.25 T magnetic field. The magnitude of the force the field exerts on one proton is F = qvB = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 4.0 × 10⁶ × 0.25 = 1.6 × 10⁻¹³ N, directed perpendicular to the velocity as the slap rule decrees.
bronowski.8: A force constant in size and forever turned at right angles to the velocity—this is precisely the condition for uniform circular motion, the very thing we met in Unit 3 AOS 1. A charge crossing a uniform magnetic field is therefore bent into a circle: the magnetic force serves as the centripetal force. Set qvB equal to mv²/r, rearrange, and the radius falls out.
bronowski.9: Four consequences follow at once, and each carries a plain meaning. A faster particle sweeps a wider circle (r ∝ v), and a stronger field bends it into a tighter one (r ∝ 1/B). A heavier particle, having more inertia to overcome, turns upon a wider arc (r ∝ m). And reverse the sign of the charge, and you reverse the force, so that electrons and protons entering the same field with the same velocity wind about in opposite senses.
bronowski.10: Upon the charge the magnetic force does no work at all. Work asks for a force with some component along the motion, and the magnetic force never offers one: at every instant it stands square to the velocity. The speed holds constant, the kinetic energy holds constant; only the direction of the motion is changed, as FIG 4.2 shows. A magnetic field can steer a charged particle but can never quicken or slow it—any change of speed must come from an electric field.
bronowski.11: Key idea A uniform magnetic field alters the direction of a moving charge and never its speed. Speed, kinetic energy and radius all hold fast; the velocity does nothing but turn.
bronowski.12: Worked example. Anika fires an electron at 2.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ into a uniform 1.5 mT field, perpendicular to the field. Set relativistic effects aside. Convert the field first: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 2.0 × 10⁷) ÷ (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 1.5 × 10⁻³) = 7.6 × 10⁻² m; the electron circles with a radius of 7.6 cm, at a speed that never alters.
bronowski.13: Trap The millitesla. Field strengths come to us again and again quoted in mT: convert them before you substitute, as in 2.5 mT = 2.5 × 10⁻³ T. Put in the bare number and the force comes out 1000 times too great and the radius 1000 times too small—and the wrong answer, worst of all, wears a plausible face.

To turn is to accelerate, and Unit 4 will show that accelerating charges radiate—which is the very reason the ring stands as a source of light. bronowski.16: n tells how many conductors lie side by side within the field: for a coil of 20 turns, n = 20, and the force is 20 times the force upon a single wire. Only the length truly inside the field contributes to l (mark the lowercase l, as it stands on the formula sheet).

The opposite side carries the same current the other way, and so it feels an equal force in the opposite direction—a pair of forces that can set the coil turning.

noether.0: Ask what a magnetic field leaves untouched and what it acts on. It exerts a force on moving charge — but only on the part of the motion that crosses the field; motion along the field is preserved, force-free. This topic covers the force law for free charges and for currents, the circular paths in which speed is conserved while direction yields, and the notation that lets us work in three dimensions.
noether.1: A stationary charge feels nothing: with no motion to cross the field, there is nothing for the field to act on. Only a moving charge experiences a force, and even then only the component of the motion that crosses the field counts — the rest is left invariant. Two cases exhaust everything in this course, and they are the two extremes of that symmetry: velocity perpendicular to the field, where the crossing is total, and velocity parallel to it, where there is no crossing at all.
noether.2: The field strength B is measured in tesla (T). The formula holds when the velocity is perpendicular to the field — the case of maximum crossing. When the charge moves parallel — or antiparallel — to the field, nothing crosses and the force is exactly zero. A proton fired along a field line has its whole velocity preserved: it travels in a straight line at constant speed.
noether.3: Trap No crossing, no force. A charge moving along the field direction is left wholly untouched — zero magnetic force, however fast it moves and however strong the field. The same invariance holds for a wire whose current runs along the field (see Force on a current-carrying wire). Applying F = qvB without first asking what angle v makes with B — without checking how much actually crosses — is a common error.
noether.4: The direction of the force is fixed by the right-hand slap rule, as shown in FIG 4.1. Hold the right hand flat with the thumb at right angles to the fingers: thumb along the velocity of a positive charge, fingers along the field — the palm then faces in the direction of the force. The force is always perpendicular to both the velocity and the field, never sharing a line with either.
noether.5: For a negative charge, the result reverses. Either apply the slap rule and flip the force, or point the thumb against the electron's motion — a negative charge moving left is, as far as the field is concerned, a positive charge moving right. This reversal recurs in every electron-beam problem; hold onto it.
noether.6: Because v, B and F are mutually perpendicular, the three cannot all lie on the page — one of them must point along the line of sight in a diagram. A field into the page is drawn as a grid of × symbols and a field out of the page as • symbols; the convention is set out in Into and out of the page.
noether.7: Worked example. Lucia studies a beam in which each proton travels at 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ at right angles to a uniform 0.25 T magnetic field — the case of full crossing. The magnitude of the force exerted on one proton by the field is F = qvB = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 4.0 × 10⁶ × 0.25 = 1.6 × 10⁻¹³ N, directed perpendicular to the velocity as the slap rule requires.
noether.8: A force of constant magnitude held forever perpendicular to the velocity is precisely the condition for uniform circular motion, met in Unit 3 AOS 1. So a charge moving across a uniform magnetic field is bound to a circle: the magnetic force is exactly the centripetal force the orbit demands. Equating qvB with mv²/r and rearranging gives the radius.
noether.9: Four consequences fall straight out of that equality. A faster particle sweeps a wider circle (r ∝ v), and a stronger field bends a tighter one (r ∝ 1/B). A more massive particle, carrying more inertia, resists the turn and so traces a wider circle (r ∝ m). And reversing the sign of the charge reverses the force, so electrons and protons that enter the same field with the same velocity circulate in opposite senses — same geometry, mirrored.
noether.10: The magnetic force does no work on the charge. Work demands a force component along the motion, and this force never has one: it is perpendicular to the velocity at every instant. Speed is therefore conserved, and with it kinetic energy; only the direction of motion yields, as shown in FIG 4.2. A magnetic field can steer a charged particle but can never change its speed — any change of speed must come from an electric field.
noether.11: Key idea A uniform magnetic field turns the direction of a moving charge while leaving its speed invariant. Speed, kinetic energy and radius are all conserved; the velocity vector only rotates.
noether.12: Worked example. Anika fires an electron at 2.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ into a uniform 1.5 mT field, perpendicular to the field. Ignore relativistic effects. Convert the field first: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 2.0 × 10⁷) ÷ (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 1.5 × 10⁻³) = 7.6 × 10⁻² m; the electron circles with a radius of 7.6 cm at a speed that is never altered.
noether.13: Trap Millitesla. Field strengths are routinely quoted in mT: convert before substituting, as in 2.5 mT = 2.5 × 10⁻³ T. Carry the raw number through and the force comes out 1000 times too large and the radius 1000 times too small — and the wrong answer wears a plausible face.
noether.14: Connects The Australian Synchrotron's storage ring is r = mv/qB at building scale: bending magnets hold electrons on a closed circular path. To turn is to accelerate, and Unit 4 will show that accelerating charges radiate — which is exactly why the ring is a light source. The full accelerator pipeline is assembled in 8 · Motors & accelerators.
noether.15: A current is charge in motion, so a wire carrying current across a magnetic field cannot escape a force: the field acts on every drifting charge, and the charges, unable to leave the metal, hand the force on to the wire itself. For a straight conductor at right angles to a uniform field — the case of full crossing:
noether.16: n counts how many conductors sit side by side in the field: for a coil of 20 turns, n = 20 and the force is 20 times the force on a single wire. Only the length actually inside the field contributes to l — what lies outside crosses nothing and counts for nothing (note the lowercase l, as on the formula sheet).
noether.17: The slap rule gives the direction, the thumb now pointing along the conventional current: thumb along I, fingers along B, palm facing along F, as shown in FIG 4.3. The zero-force case returns here in full force: if the current runs parallel or antiparallel to the field, the force is zero. In a rectangular coil lying in a field, the two sides whose current crosses the field feel forces; the two sides whose current runs along the field feel none. That single asymmetry is the key to the DC motor in the next topic.
noether.18: Worked example. Magnus tests a flat coil of 20 turns. One side of the coil is 0.050 m long, carries a current of 1.5 A and lies at right angles to a uniform 0.30 T field. The magnitude of the force exerted on that side by the field is F = nIlB = 20 × 1.5 × 0.050 × 0.30 = 0.45 N. The opposite side carries the same current the opposite way, so it feels an equal force in the opposite direction — an equal-and-opposite pair whose net effect is a turning of the coil.
noether.19: Connects This section is the motor principle: a magnetic field makes a current-carrying coil move. Unit 3 AOS 3 runs the same relation in reverse — moving a coil through a field drives a current — and that reversal, electromagnetic induction via changing magnetic flux, is how every generator in the grid works.
noether.20: Magnetic-force problems are irreducibly three-dimensional: v (or I), B and F are mutually perpendicular, so all three can never lie on the page at once. The standard convention sacrifices one of them to the line of sight and pictures it as an arrow seen end-on: from behind, the crossed fletching of the tail — ×, into the page; head-on as it approaches, the point — •, out of the page. Every diagram that uses the symbols should carry a key, for example "× = field into the page".
noether.21: A reliable routine for any direction question, taken in order. First, read the field direction off the symbols. Second, point the flat right hand's thumb along the velocity of positive charge — reversed for an electron — or along the conventional current. Third, align the fingers with the field; the palm now faces along the force.
noether.22: For example, an electron travels up the page through a field that points out of the page (•). For a positive charge moving up the page, the slap rule gives a force to the right; the electron's negative charge reverses this, so the force exerted on the electron by the field is to the left of the page.

mack.0: A magnetic field shoves moving charge around — but it’s picky: it only pushes on motion that actually crosses the field. This topic works out the force law for free charges and for currents, the looping circular paths that come out of it, and the neat shorthand for drawing all of this in three dimensions on a flat page.
mack.1: Leave a charge sitting still and a magnetic field does nothing to it — not a nudge. Only a moving charge feels a force, and even then only the slice of its motion that cuts across the field counts. Two cases cover absolutely everything you’ll meet in this course: velocity at right angles to the field, and velocity running straight along it.
mack.2: The field strength B is measured in tesla (T). The formula holds when the velocity is perpendicular to the field. Send the charge moving parallel to the field — or antiparallel — and the force vanishes completely, exactly zero. Fire a proton straight along a field line and it just keeps going: a straight line at constant speed, as if the field weren’t there at all.
mack.3: Trap No crossing, no force. A charge sliding along the field direction feels zero magnetic force, no matter how fast it moves or how brutal the field. Same deal for a wire whose current runs along the field (see Force on a current-carrying wire). Reaching for F = qvB before you’ve checked the angle between v and B is one of the classic ways to go wrong here.
mack.4: Which way does the force point? The right-hand slap rule tells you, as shown in FIG 4.1. Flatten your right hand with the thumb stuck out at right angles to the fingers: thumb along the velocity of a positive charge, fingers along the field — and your palm now faces the way the force pushes. That force is always perpendicular to both the velocity and the field, every time.
mack.5: Got a negative charge? Flip the answer. Either run the slap rule as usual and reverse the force, or aim your thumb against the electron’s motion — a negative charge heading left behaves just like a positive charge heading right. That flip shows up in every single electron-beam problem, so make peace with it now.
mack.6: Since v, B and F are all mutually perpendicular, one of the three usually ends up pointing straight along your line of sight in a diagram — in or out of the page. A field into the page gets drawn as a grid of × symbols and a field out of the page as • symbols; the full convention is laid out in Into and out of the page.
mack.7: Worked example. Lucia studies a beam in which each proton travels at 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ at right angles to a uniform 0.25 T magnetic field. The magnitude of the force the field exerts on one proton is F = qvB = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 4.0 × 10⁶ × 0.25 = 1.6 × 10⁻¹³ N, pointing perpendicular to the velocity, direction handed to you by the slap rule.
mack.8: A force that keeps a constant size while always pointing perpendicular to the velocity — that’s the exact recipe for uniform circular motion, the same one you met back in Unit 3 AOS 1. So a charge cutting across a uniform magnetic field bends into a circle: the magnetic force is the centripetal force, doing the whole job. Set qvB equal to mv²/r, rearrange, and out drops the radius.
mack.9: Four payoffs fall straight out of that. Speed the particle up and the circle widens (r ∝ v); crank up the field and the circle tightens (r ∝ 1/B). Make the particle heavier — more inertia, harder to turn — and it swings around a wider circle (r ∝ m). And flip the sign of the charge and you flip the force, so electrons and protons entering the same field with the same velocity wheel around in opposite directions.
mack.10: Here’s the elegant part: the magnetic force does no work on the charge. Work needs a force component pointing along the motion, and the magnetic force never has one — it sits perpendicular to the velocity at every single instant. The speed stays put, the kinetic energy stays put; only the direction of motion changes, as shown in FIG 4.2. A magnetic field can steer a charged particle wherever it likes, but it can’t speed it up or slow it down — any change of speed has to come from an electric field.
mack.11: Key idea A uniform magnetic field changes the direction of a moving charge and nothing else — never its speed. Speed, kinetic energy and radius all hold steady; the velocity just keeps turning.
mack.12: Worked example. Anika fires an electron at 2.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ into a uniform 1.5 mT field, perpendicular to the field. Ignore relativistic effects. Sort the field out first: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 2.0 × 10⁷) ÷ (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 1.5 × 10⁻³) = 7.6 × 10⁻² m; so the electron loops around with a radius of 7.6 cm, at a speed that never budges.
mack.13: Trap Millitesla. Field strengths get quoted in mT all the time — convert before you substitute, as in 2.5 mT = 2.5 × 10⁻³ T. Plug in the bare number and you’ll make the force 1000 times too big and the radius 1000 times too small, and the cruelest part is that the wrong answer still looks perfectly reasonable.
mack.14: Connects The Australian Synchrotron’s storage ring is just r = mv/qB blown up to building scale: bending magnets pin electrons onto a closed circular path. Turning means accelerating, and Unit 4 will show that accelerating charges radiate — which is precisely why the ring is a light source in the first place. The full accelerator pipeline gets bolted together in 8 · Motors & accelerators.
mack.15: A current is just charge on the move, so a wire carrying current across a magnetic field feels a force too: the field pushes on every drifting charge, and since those charges can’t escape the metal, they hand the push straight to the wire. For a straight conductor at right angles to a uniform field:
mack.16: n counts how many conductors sit shoulder to shoulder in the field: for a coil of 20 turns, n = 20 and the force is 20 times the force on a lone wire. And only the length actually inside the field pulls its weight in l (mind the lowercase l, just as it appears on the formula sheet).
mack.17: The slap rule hands you the direction again, except now the thumb points along the conventional current: thumb along I, fingers along B, palm facing along F, as shown in FIG 4.3. And the zero-force case comes roaring back: if the current runs parallel or antiparallel to the field, the force is zero. Lay a rectangular coil in a field and the two sides whose current crosses the field feel forces; the two sides whose current runs along the field feel nothing. Hold onto that one fact — it’s the whole secret behind the DC motor in the next topic.
mack.18: Worked example. Magnus tests a flat coil of 20 turns. One side of the coil is 0.050 m long, carries a current of 1.5 A and lies at right angles to a uniform 0.30 T field. The magnitude of the force the field exerts on that side is F = nIlB = 20 × 1.5 × 0.050 × 0.30 = 0.45 N. The opposite side carries the same current the other way, so it feels an equal force in the opposite direction — a matched pair of forces, and that pair is exactly what can spin the coil.
mack.19: Connects This whole section is the motor principle: a magnetic field sets a current-carrying coil moving. Unit 3 AOS 3 runs the same idea backwards — drag a coil through a field and you drive a current — and that reversal, electromagnetic induction through changing magnetic flux, is how every generator on the grid earns its keep.
mack.20: Magnetic-force problems are stubbornly three-dimensional: v (or I), B and F are mutually perpendicular, so there’s no way to fit all three flat on the page at once. The trick is to send one of them along your line of sight and draw it as an arrow you’re viewing end-on: seen from behind, you spot the crossed fletching of the tail — ×, into the page; seen head-on, you spot the point — •, out of the page. Every diagram that leans on these symbols should carry a key, something like "× = field into the page".
mack.21: A routine that won’t let you down on any direction question: first, read the field direction straight off the symbols. Second, flatten your right hand and aim the thumb along the velocity of positive charge — reversed for an electron — or along the conventional current. Third, line the fingers up with the field; your palm is now facing along the force. Done.
mack.22: Try one. An electron travels up the page through a field pointing out of the page (•). For a positive charge heading up the page, the slap rule serves up a force to the right; but the electron’s negative charge flips that, so the force the field exerts on the electron actually points to the left of the page.

levin.0: A magnetic field reaches out and pushes on moving charge — but only on the motion that dares to cross it. Travel along the field and nothing happens; cut across it and you feel the hand. This topic follows that force through free charges and through currents, watches the circles it carves, and learns the small notation we need to think in three dimensions at once.
levin.1: Hold a charge still in a magnetic field and it feels nothing at all. Only a moving charge is touched, and even then only the part of its motion that crosses the field is counted — the rest is invisible to the force. Two cases hold everything this course will ask of you: velocity perpendicular to the field, and velocity running parallel to it.
levin.2: The strength of the field, B, is measured in tesla (T). The formula speaks only when the velocity meets the field at a right angle. Move the charge parallel to the field — or straight against it — and the force falls to exactly zero. A proton fired along a field line simply keeps going: a straight line, an unchanging speed, no turning at all.
levin.3: Trap No crossing, no force. A charge that travels along the field direction feels nothing from it, however fast it flies and however fierce the field. The same holds for a wire whose current runs along the field (see Force on a current-carrying wire). Reaching for F = qvB before you have looked at the angle between v and B is the error that catches the unwary.
levin.4: The direction is given to you by the right-hand slap rule, drawn in FIG 4.1. Lay the right hand flat, thumb held square to the fingers: thumb along the velocity of a positive charge, fingers lying down the field — and the palm turns to face the way the force pushes. That force stands always perpendicular to both the velocity and the field; it never leans toward either.
levin.5: For a negative charge, turn the answer around. Either work the slap rule and then flip the force, or aim the thumb against the electron's motion — for a negative charge drifting left is, to the field, a positive charge drifting right. This single reversal lives inside every electron-beam problem you will meet.
levin.6: Because v, B and F stand mutually perpendicular, one of the three almost always points straight down your line of sight, off the flat of the page. A field into the page is written as a grid of × symbols, a field out of the page as •; the convention is laid out in Into and out of the page.
levin.7: Worked example. Lucia studies a beam in which each proton travels at 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ at right angles to a uniform 0.25 T magnetic field. The magnitude of the force the field exerts on one proton is F = qvB = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 4.0 × 10⁶ × 0.25 = 1.6 × 10⁻¹³ N, pushing perpendicular to the velocity, the way the slap rule promises.
levin.8: A force whose magnitude never changes, forever turned at a right angle to the motion — that is precisely the recipe for uniform circular motion, the one you met in Unit 3 AOS 1. So a charge crossing a uniform field bends into a circle, the magnetic force standing in as the centripetal force that holds it there. Set qvB equal to mv²/r, rearrange, and the radius falls out.
levin.9: Four consequences follow, one after another. A faster particle sweeps a wider circle (r ∝ v); a stronger field bends a tighter one (r ∝ 1/B). A heavier particle, carrying more inertia, turns on a wider arc (r ∝ m). And reverse the sign of the charge and you reverse the force itself — so an electron and a proton, entering the same field with the same velocity, wheel in opposite directions, mirror images circling apart.
levin.10: On the charge, the magnetic force does no work — none at all. Work asks for a force that leans along the motion, and the magnetic force never leans: it holds perpendicular to the velocity at every single instant. The speed stays fixed, the kinetic energy stays fixed; only the direction wheels around, as FIG 4.2 shows. A magnetic field can steer a charged particle, but it can never hurry or slow it — any change in speed must come from an electric field.
levin.11: Key idea A uniform magnetic field turns the direction of a moving charge and never its speed. Speed, kinetic energy and radius all hold constant; only the velocity wheels around.
levin.12: Worked example. Anika fires an electron at 2.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ into a uniform 1.5 mT field, square to the field. Set relativistic effects aside. Convert the field first: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 2.0 × 10⁷) ÷ (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 1.5 × 10⁻³) = 7.6 × 10⁻² m; the electron circles with a radius of 7.6 cm, at a speed that never once changes.
levin.13: Trap Millitesla. Field strengths are quoted in mT as a matter of habit: convert before you substitute, as in 2.5 mT = 2.5 × 10⁻³ T. Drop in the raw number and the force comes out 1000 times too large, the radius 1000 times too small — and the wrong answer wears a perfectly plausible face.
levin.14: Connects The Australian Synchrotron's storage ring is r = mv/qB written at the scale of a building: bending magnets hold electrons fast on a closed circular path. To turn is to accelerate, and Unit 4 will show that accelerating charges radiate — which is the whole reason the ring shines as a light source. The full accelerator pipeline is assembled in 8 · Motors & accelerators.
levin.15: A current is nothing but charge in motion, so a wire carrying current across a magnetic field is pushed too: the field leans on every drifting charge, and the charges, trapped inside the metal and unable to escape, hand that push on to the wire itself. For a straight conductor held at right angles to a uniform field:
levin.16: The symbol n simply counts how many conductors sit side by side within the field: for a coil of 20 turns, n = 20, and the force grows to 20 times the force on a lone wire. Only the length that actually lies inside the field is allowed to count toward l (note the lowercase l, as it stands on the formula sheet).
levin.17: The slap rule hands you the direction again, the thumb now lying along the conventional current: thumb along I, fingers along B, palm facing along F, as FIG 4.3 shows. And the zero-force case returns in full force: if the current runs parallel or antiparallel to the field, the force is zero. In a rectangular coil lying in a field, the two sides whose current crosses the field are pushed; the two sides whose current runs along the field feel nothing. That one fact is the seed of the DC motor in the next topic.
levin.18: Worked example. Magnus tests a flat coil of 20 turns. One side of the coil is 0.050 m long, carries a current of 1.5 A and lies at right angles to a uniform 0.30 T field. The magnitude of the force the field exerts on that side is F = nIlB = 20 × 1.5 × 0.050 × 0.30 = 0.45 N. The opposite side carries the same current the opposite way, so it feels an equal force in the opposite direction — a pair of forces, pulling against each other, ready to turn the coil.
levin.19: Connects This is the motor principle in its bones: a magnetic field sets a current-carrying coil moving. Unit 3 AOS 3 runs the same story backwards — move a coil through a field and a current is born — and that reversal, electromagnetic induction through changing magnetic flux, is how every generator on the grid keeps the lights on.
levin.20: Magnetic-force problems live in three dimensions: v (or I), B and F stand mutually perpendicular, so all three can never lie flat on the page together. The convention sends one of them down the line of sight and pictures it as an arrow: seen from behind, the crossed fletching of its tail — ×, into the page; seen rushing toward you, the point — •, out of the page. Every diagram that leans on these symbols should carry a key, for example "× = field into the page".

Second, lay the flat right hand's thumb along the velocity of positive charge — reversed for an electron — or along the conventional current. Third, bring the fingers into line with the field; the palm now faces the way the force pushes.

For a positive charge moving up the page, the slap rule swings the force to the right; the electron's negative charge turns that around, so the force the field exerts on the electron points to the left of the page.

Hold your right hand flat, thumb cocked out at right angles to the fingers: point the thumb along the velocity of a positive charge and the fingers along the field, and your palm ends up facing the way the force pushes.
