Third, line the fingers up with the field; your palm is now facing along the force.
czerski.22: Take an example: an electron heads up the page through a field pointing out of the page (•). For a positive charge going up the page, the slap rule gives a force to the right; the electron’s negative charge turns that around, so the force the field lands on the electron points to the left of the page.

veritasium.0: You might think a magnet pulls on anything electric — that a charge sitting near it feels a tug. It doesn't. A magnetic field ignores a charge completely until that charge starts moving, and even then it only grabs the part of the motion that cuts across the field. This topic follows that one strange rule everywhere it leads: the force on a free charge, the force on a current, the circular paths charges get trapped in, and the notation you need to keep all of it straight in three dimensions.
veritasium.1: Park a charge in a magnetic field and nothing happens — no force at all. The field only wakes up when the charge moves, and even then it's pickier than you'd expect: it only responds to the part of the motion that crosses the field. Two cases cover everything you'll meet in this course, and they sit at opposite extremes — velocity straight across the field, and velocity straight along it.
veritasium.2: The field strength B is measured in tesla (T). This formula only works when the velocity is perpendicular to the field — square across it. You might assume a charge running along the field at least feels something, even a little. It doesn't: move parallel, or antiparallel, to the field and the force is exactly zero. Fire a proton straight along a field line and it just coasts — a straight line, constant speed, as if the field weren't there.
veritasium.3: Trap No crossing, no force. A charge sliding along the field direction feels zero magnetic force — and it doesn't matter how fast it's going or how monstrously strong the field is. The same is true for a wire whose current runs along the field (see Force on a current-carrying wire). The classic mistake is to reach straight for F = qvB and start plugging in numbers, before ever checking the angle between v and B.
veritasium.4: So which way does the force point? Your hand will tell you, using the right-hand slap rule shown in FIG 4.1. Hold the right hand flat with the thumb at right angles to the fingers: thumb along the velocity of a positive charge, fingers along the field — and the palm now faces exactly the way the force pushes. Notice where it points: always perpendicular to both the velocity and the field, off in a direction neither of them was heading.
veritasium.5: For a negative charge, everything flips. You've got two ways to handle it: run the slap rule as normal and then reverse the force, or point your thumb against the electron's motion in the first place — because a negative charge drifting left behaves exactly like a positive charge drifting right. That flip is hiding inside every electron-beam problem you'll ever do, so it's worth getting into your fingers now.
veritasium.6: Here's the catch with drawing any of this. Because v, B and F all sit at right angles to each other, one of them almost always points straight at you, or straight away — right along your line of sight, where an arrow on the page can't show it. A field into the page gets drawn as a grid of × symbols, a field out of the page as • symbols; the full convention is laid out in Into and out of the page.
veritasium.7: Worked example. Lucia studies a beam in which each proton travels at 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ at right angles to a uniform 0.25 T magnetic field. The magnitude of the force exerted on one proton by the field is F = qvB = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 4.0 × 10⁶ × 0.25 = 1.6 × 10⁻¹³ N, pointing perpendicular to the velocity in the direction the slap rule hands you.
veritasium.8: Now look at what that force actually does. A force that keeps the same size but always pulls perpendicular to the velocity — that's the exact recipe for uniform circular motion you met in Unit 3 AOS 1. So a charge cutting across a uniform magnetic field has no choice: it curves into a circle, with the magnetic force playing the role of the centripetal force. Set qvB equal to mv²/r, rearrange, and the radius falls out.
veritasium.9: Four things drop straight out of that, and they're more intuitive than they look. Speed the particle up and it can't turn as sharply, so the circle widens (r ∝ v). Crank up the field and it bends harder, so the circle tightens (r ∝ 1/B). Make the particle heavier and its extra inertia resists the turn, swinging it round a wider circle (r ∝ m). And flip the sign of the charge and you flip the force — so an electron and a proton fired into the same field with the same velocity wind round in opposite directions.
veritasium.10: Here's the part that trips people up: the magnetic force does no work on the charge. None. To do work, a force needs a component pointing along the motion — and the magnetic force never has one, because it's perpendicular to the velocity at every single instant. So the speed stays fixed, the kinetic energy stays fixed, and only the direction changes, as shown in FIG 4.2. A magnetic field can steer a charged particle anywhere you like, but it can never speed it up or slow it down — for that you need an electric field.
veritasium.11: Key idea A uniform magnetic field turns a moving charge without ever changing its speed. Speed, kinetic energy and radius all hold steady — the only thing that ever changes is which way the velocity points.
veritasium.12: Worked example. Anika fires an electron at 2.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ into a uniform 1.5 mT field, perpendicular to the field. Ignore relativistic effects. Convert the field first: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 2.0 × 10⁷) ÷ (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 1.5 × 10⁻³) = 7.6 × 10⁻² m; the electron loops round with a radius of 7.6 cm, at a speed that never budges.
veritasium.13: Trap Millitesla. Field strengths get quoted in mT all the time, and it's easy to forget to convert — but you have to, before anything goes into the formula, like 2.5 mT = 2.5 × 10⁻³ T. Drop the raw number in and your force comes out 1000 times too big and your radius 1000 times too small — and the worst part is the wrong answer still looks perfectly reasonable, so nothing flags the mistake.
veritasium.14: Connects The Australian Synchrotron's storage ring is just r = mv/qB blown up to the size of a building: bending magnets pin electrons onto a closed circular loop. But remember — turning is accelerating, and Unit 4 will reveal that accelerating charges throw off radiation. That's not a side effect, it's the whole point: it's exactly why the ring works as a light source. The full accelerator pipeline gets assembled in 8 · Motors & accelerators.
veritasium.15: A current is nothing but charge on the move, so here's the consequence: run a wire carrying current across a magnetic field and the wire itself gets pushed. The field grabs every drifting charge inside, and since those charges can't escape the metal, they drag the whole wire along with them. For a straight conductor sitting at right angles to a uniform field:
veritasium.16: n just counts how many conductors are lined up side by side in the field: wind a coil of 20 turns and n = 20, so the force is 20 times what a single wire would feel. And watch out for the length — only the stretch actually inside the field counts toward l (note the lowercase l, the way it appears on the formula sheet).
veritasium.17: The slap rule still tells you the direction — just point the thumb along the conventional current this time: thumb along I, fingers along B, palm facing along F, as shown in FIG 4.3. And the zero-force case comes roaring back, full strength: if the current runs parallel or antiparallel to the field, the force is zero. Lay a rectangular coil in a field and you'll see it split — the two sides whose current crosses the field get pushed, the two sides whose current runs along the field feel nothing. Hold onto that one fact, because it's the whole secret of the DC motor in the next topic.
veritasium.18: Worked example. Magnus tests a flat coil of 20 turns. One side of the coil is 0.050 m long, carries a current of 1.5 A and lies at right angles to a uniform 0.30 T field. The magnitude of the force exerted on that side by the field is F = nIlB = 20 × 1.5 × 0.050 × 0.30 = 0.45 N. The opposite side carries the same current the other way, so it feels an equal force in the opposite direction — and that pair of opposing pushes is exactly what can spin the coil round.
veritasium.19: Connects What you've just built is the motor principle: a magnetic field shoves a current-carrying coil into motion. Now run the whole thing backwards — that's Unit 3 AOS 3, where dragging a coil through a field drives a current instead. That reversal, electromagnetic induction through changing magnetic flux, is the trick behind every generator feeding the grid.
veritasium.20: Magnetic-force problems are stubbornly three-dimensional: v (or I), B and F all sit at right angles, so there's no way to fit all three flat on the page at once. The standard fix is to send one of them straight along your line of sight and draw it as an arrow seen end-on: look at the tail of the arrow flying away and you see the crossed fletching — ×, into the page; look at it coming at you and you see the point — •, out of the page. Any diagram using the symbols should spell it out with a key, for example "× = field into the page".
veritasium.21: Here's a routine that never fails you on a direction question. First, read the field direction straight off the symbols. Second, lay your flat right hand's thumb along the velocity of positive charge — and reverse it for an electron — or along the conventional current. Third, line the fingers up with the field, and the palm now points the way the force pushes. That's it, every time.
veritasium.22: Try it: an electron heads up the page through a field pointing out of the page (•). Run the slap rule for a positive charge moving up the page and it gives a force to the right. But the electron is negative, so flip it — the force the field exerts on the electron points to the left of the page.

attenborough.0: A magnetic field reaches out and presses upon a moving charge — but only, and this is the great secret, upon motion that dares to cross the field. Here we shall follow the force as it falls upon free charges and upon currents alike, watch the curving paths that obey it, and learn the quiet notation by which we navigate all three dimensions.
attenborough.1: Upon a charge that rests, a magnetic field lays no hand at all. Only a charge in motion feels the force — and only that part of its motion which crosses the field is counted. Two cases, and two alone, will carry us through everything in this course: a velocity that runs perpendicular to the field, and a velocity that runs along it.
attenborough.2: The strength of the field, B, we measure in tesla (T). The formula holds when the velocity meets the field at right angles. But should the charge drift parallel to the field — or directly against it — the force vanishes utterly, to exactly zero. A proton fired along a field line journeys onward in a straight line, its speed never wavering.
attenborough.3: Trap No crossing, no force. A charge that travels along the field direction feels not the slightest magnetic force — however swiftly it moves, however mighty the field. The same fate awaits a wire whose current runs along the field (see Force on a current-carrying wire). To reach for F = qvB without first pausing to study the angle between v and B — that is the error into which so many stumble.
attenborough.4: The direction of the force is revealed by the right-hand slap rule, as we see in FIG 4.1. Hold the right hand open and flat, the thumb set at right angles to the fingers: the thumb along the velocity of a positive charge, the fingers along the field — and now the palm itself faces the way of the force. Always, without exception, that force stands perpendicular to both the velocity and the field.
attenborough.5: For a negative charge, the result must be turned about. Either apply the slap rule and reverse the force, or aim the thumb against the electron's motion: a negative charge drifting left is, to the field, indistinguishable from a positive charge drifting right. This quiet reversal lies at the heart of every electron-beam problem.
attenborough.6: Because v, B and F stand mutually perpendicular, one of the three will, as a rule, point straight along the line of sight in any diagram. A field running into the page we draw as a grid of × symbols, and a field rising out of the page as • symbols; the convention is laid out in Into and out of the page.
attenborough.7: Worked example. Lucia studies a beam in which each proton travels at 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ at right angles to a uniform 0.25 T magnetic field. The magnitude of the force the field exerts upon a single proton is F = qvB = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 4.0 × 10⁶ × 0.25 = 1.6 × 10⁻¹³ N, directed perpendicular to the velocity, just as the slap rule foretells.
attenborough.8: A force of unchanging magnitude, forever perpendicular to the velocity — this is the very condition for uniform circular motion, which we first met in Unit 3 AOS 1. And so a charge crossing a uniform magnetic field is drawn into a circle: the magnetic force becomes the centripetal force that holds it there. Set qvB equal to mv²/r, rearrange, and the radius emerges.
attenborough.9: Four consequences follow, each at once. A swifter particle sweeps a wider circle (r ∝ v), and a stronger field bends a tighter one (r ∝ 1/B). A heavier particle, carrying more inertia, turns upon a wider arc (r ∝ m). And reverse the sign of the charge, and you reverse the force itself — so that electrons and protons, entering the same field with the same velocity, circle forever in opposite directions.
attenborough.10: Upon the charge, the magnetic force does no work at all. For work demands a force that pulls along the direction of motion, and the magnetic force never does: at every instant it stands perpendicular to the velocity. The speed holds constant, the kinetic energy holds constant; only the direction of travel turns, as shown in FIG 4.2. A magnetic field may steer a charged particle, yet it can never quicken or slow it — any change of speed must come instead from an electric field.
attenborough.11: Key idea A uniform magnetic field changes the direction of a moving charge, and never its speed. Speed, kinetic energy and radius all hold fast; the velocity only turns.
attenborough.12: Worked example. Anika fires an electron at 2.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ into a uniform 1.5 mT field, perpendicular to the field. Set aside, for now, the effects of relativity. First the field must be converted: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 2.0 × 10⁷) ÷ (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 1.5 × 10⁻³) = 7.6 × 10⁻² m; the electron wheels round on a radius of 7.6 cm, at a speed that never, ever changes.
attenborough.13: Trap Millitesla. Field strengths come to us, time and again, quoted in mT: convert them before you substitute, as in 2.5 mT = 2.5 × 10⁻³ T. Substitute the raw number and the force swells a thousandfold too great, the radius shrinks a thousandfold too small — and the wrong answer, deceptively, looks every bit as plausible as the right one.
attenborough.14: Connects The Australian Synchrotron's storage ring is nothing less than r = mv/qB raised to the scale of a building: bending magnets hold the electrons fast upon a closed circular path. To turn is to accelerate, and Unit 4 will reveal that accelerating charges radiate light — which is the very reason the ring shines as a light source at all. The whole accelerator pipeline is assembled in 8 · Motors & accelerators.
attenborough.15: A current is charge in motion, and so a wire that carries current across a magnetic field must feel a force: the field falls upon every drifting charge, and those charges, unable to escape the metal, hand the force on to the wire itself. For a straight conductor set at right angles to a uniform field:
attenborough.16: n tells us how many conductors lie side by side within the field: for a coil of 20 turns, n = 20, and the force grows to 20 times the force upon a single wire. Only the length truly within the field plays its part in l (note the lowercase l, just as it appears on the formula sheet).
attenborough.17: Again the slap rule reveals the direction, the thumb now aimed along the conventional current: thumb along I, fingers along B, palm facing along F, as shown in FIG 4.3. And here the zero-force case returns to us in full: if the current runs parallel or antiparallel to the field, the force is zero. In a rectangular coil resting within a field, the two sides whose current crosses the field feel forces; the two whose current runs along the field feel nothing at all. That single fact, modest as it seems, is the key to the DC motor we meet in the next topic.
attenborough.18: Worked example. Magnus tests a flat coil of 20 turns. One side of the coil is 0.050 m long, carries a current of 1.5 A and lies at right angles to a uniform 0.30 T field. The magnitude of the force the field exerts upon that side is F = nIlB = 20 × 1.5 × 0.050 × 0.30 = 0.45 N. The opposite side carries the same current the opposite way, and so it feels an equal force in the opposite direction — a pair of forces that, together, can set the coil turning.
attenborough.19: Connects Here, in this section, lies the very principle of the motor: a magnetic field sets a current-carrying coil in motion. Unit 3 AOS 3 runs the same story in reverse — move a coil through a field and a current is born — and that reversal, electromagnetic induction through changing magnetic flux, is how every generator in the grid does its work.
attenborough.20: Problems of magnetic force are, by their nature, three-dimensional: v (or I), B and F stand mutually perpendicular, so all three can never lie upon the page at once. The standard convention sets one of them along the line of sight and pictures it as an arrow: seen from behind, the crossed fletching of its tail — ×, into the page; seen rushing toward us, the point — •, out of the page. Every diagram that calls upon these symbols should carry a key, for instance "× = field into the page".
attenborough.21: A trustworthy routine for any question of direction. First, read the field direction from the symbols. Second, set the thumb of the flat right hand along the velocity of positive charge — reversed, of course, for an electron — or along the conventional current. Third, lay the fingers along the field; and now the palm faces along the force.
attenborough.22: Consider, for example, an electron travelling up the page through a field that points out of the page (•). For a positive charge moving up the page, the slap rule gives a force to the right; but the electron's negative charge turns this about, and so the force the field exerts upon the electron points to the left of the page.

greekepic.0: Sing, Muse, of the unseen field and its dominion over the wanderers of charge: for a magnetic field lays its hand upon a moving charge — yet only upon that motion which dares to cross the field. This song tells the law of force for the free-roaming charges and for the rivers of current, the circling paths that follow as fate, and the runes by which mortals labour in the three dimensions of the world.
greekepic.1: Upon a charge that rests, untravelled, the magnetic field lays no force at all. Only the charge that moves shall feel the field’s touch, and only that portion of its motion which crosses the field is counted in the reckoning. Two cases alone girdle the whole of this course: the velocity set crosswise to the field, and the velocity laid along it.
greekepic.2: The strength of the field, B, is measured in tesla (T). This law holds its faith when the velocity stands perpendicular to the field. But when the charge journeys along the field — or against it, antiparallel — the force is nothing, utterly zero. A proton hurled down a field line travels a straight road at constant speed, untouched and undeterred.
greekepic.3: Trap Mark well: no crossing, no force. A charge that runs along the field’s own course feels no magnetic force whatsoever, though it fly never so swift and the field stand never so mighty. The same decree binds a wire whose current flows along the field (see Force on a current-carrying wire). To wield F = qvB without first asking the angle between v and B is the error into which the heedless forever fall.
greekepic.4: The bearing of the force is revealed by the right-hand slap rule, as shown in FIG 4.1. Hold the right hand flat, the thumb set at right angles to the fingers: the thumb along the velocity of a positive charge, the fingers along the field — and the palm shall face toward the force. The force stands ever perpendicular to both the velocity and the field, beholden to neither yet ruling both.
greekepic.5: For a charge that bears the negative sign, turn the verdict about. Either work the slap rule and overturn the force, or set the thumb against the electron’s own going: a negative charge journeying leftward is as a positive charge journeying rightward. This reversal is woven into every tale of the electron-beam.
greekepic.6: Because v, B and F stand each perpendicular to the others, one of the three commonly points along the very line of the watcher’s sight in a diagram. A field driven into the page is drawn as a host of × symbols, and a field rising out of the page as • symbols; this convention is set forth in Into and out of the page.
greekepic.7: Worked example. Lucia studies a beam in which each proton travels at 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ at right angles to a uniform 0.25 T magnetic field. The magnitude of the force the field lays upon a single proton is F = qvB = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 4.0 × 10⁶ × 0.25 = 1.6 × 10⁻¹³ N, directed perpendicular to the velocity as the slap rule decrees.
greekepic.8: A force of constant magnitude, forever set perpendicular to the velocity, is the very condition of uniform circular motion, met aforetime in Unit 3 AOS 1. A charge crossing a uniform magnetic field therefore treads a circle: the magnetic force serves as the centripetal force that holds it to its round. Setting qvB equal to mv²/r and rearranging yields the radius.
greekepic.9: Four consequences follow as surely as day follows night. A swifter particle sweeps a wider circle (r ∝ v), and a mightier field bends a tighter circle (r ∝ 1/B). A weightier particle, freighted with more inertia, wheels upon a wider circle (r ∝ m). And to reverse the sign of the charge is to reverse the force, so that electrons and protons entering the same field with the same velocity circle in opposite courses, each shunning the other’s way.
greekepic.10: Upon the charge the magnetic force does no work at all. Work asks a force that bears some part along the motion, and the magnetic force never bears one: it stands perpendicular to the velocity at every instant of the journey. The speed is held constant and the kinetic energy held constant; only the bearing of the motion turns, as shown in FIG 4.2. A magnetic field may steer a charged wanderer, but it cannot quicken nor slow it — any change of speed must come from an electric field.
greekepic.11: Key idea A uniform magnetic field turns the bearing of a moving charge, never its speed. Speed, kinetic energy and radius all keep their faith unbroken; the velocity does naught but turn.
greekepic.12: Worked example. Anika fires an electron at 2.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ into a uniform 1.5 mT field, perpendicular to the field. Heed not the relativistic effects. First convert the field: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 2.0 × 10⁷) ÷ (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 1.5 × 10⁻³) = 7.6 × 10⁻² m; the electron wheels with a radius of 7.6 cm at a speed that never alters.
greekepic.13: Trap Beware the millitesla. Field strengths are commonly proclaimed in mT: convert before you substitute, as in 2.5 mT = 2.5 × 10⁻³ T. To set down the raw number makes the force a thousandfold too great and the radius a thousandfold too small, and the false answer that follows wears a plausible face to deceive the unwary.
greekepic.14: Connects The storage ring of the Australian Synchrotron is the law r = mv/qB raised to the scale of a great hall: bending magnets bind the electrons to a closed circular road. To turn is to accelerate, and Unit 4 will reveal that accelerating charges pour forth radiation — which is the very reason the ring shines as a source of light. The whole engine of the accelerator is assembled in 8 · Motors & accelerators.
greekepic.15: A current is charge set in motion, so a wire bearing current across a magnetic field shall feel a force: the field works upon every drifting charge, and the charges, unable to flee the metal, deliver the force unto the wire itself. For a straight conductor set at right angles to a uniform field:
greekepic.16: The number n tells how many conductors stand side by side within the field: for a coil of 20 turns, n = 20 and the force is twentyfold the force upon a single wire. Only the length that lies truly within the field is counted toward l (mark the lowercase l, as it stands upon the formula sheet).
greekepic.17: The slap rule grants the bearing, the thumb now set along the conventional current: thumb along I, fingers along B, palm facing along F, as shown in FIG 4.3. Here the case of zero force returns in full might: if the current runs parallel or antiparallel to the field, the force is zero. In a rectangular coil laid within a field, the two sides whose current crosses the field shall feel forces; the two sides whose current runs along the field shall feel none. That single truth is the key that unlocks the DC motor of the next topic.
greekepic.18: Worked example. Magnus tests a flat coil of 20 turns. One side of the coil is 0.050 m long, carries a current of 1.5 A and lies at right angles to a uniform 0.30 T field. The magnitude of the force the field lays upon that side is F = nIlB = 20 × 1.5 × 0.050 × 0.30 = 0.45 N. The opposite side bears the same current the opposite way, so it feels a force equal in measure and contrary in bearing — a pair of forces that can set the coil to turning.
greekepic.19: Connects This passage is the very principle of the motor: a magnetic field bids a current-bearing coil to move. Unit 3 AOS 3 runs the same tale backward — to move a coil through a field is to drive forth a current — and that reversal, electromagnetic induction wrought through changing magnetic flux, is how every generator in the grid does its work.
greekepic.20: Problems of magnetic force dwell in the three dimensions: v (or I), B and F stand each perpendicular to the others, so all three can never lie upon the page at once. The honoured convention sets one of them along the line of the watcher’s sight and pictures it as an arrow: beheld from behind, the crossed fletching of the tail — ×, into the page; beheld as it draws near, the point — •, out of the page. Every diagram that calls upon these symbols should bear a key, for instance “× = field into the page”.
greekepic.21: A trusty rite for any question of bearing: first, read the field’s direction from the symbols. Second, set the flat right hand’s thumb along the velocity of positive charge — turned about for an electron — or along the conventional current. Third, lay the fingers along the field; the palm shall then face toward the force.
greekepic.22: For example, an electron travels up the page through a field that rises out of the page (•). For a positive charge moving up the page, the slap rule yields a force to the right; the electron’s negative charge overturns this, so the force the field lays upon the electron is to the left of the page.

cnpop.0: 注意看，磁场会对运动的电荷下狠手——但有个前提：你的运动得切过磁场，沿着磁场溜达它根本懒得理你。这一节我们就来盘一盘自由电荷和电流各自吃到的力、由此画出的圆形轨迹，还有在三维空间里干活时要用的那套记号。
cnpop.1: 磁场对一个躺平不动的电荷？零作用，连看都不看一眼。只有动起来的电荷才会挨力，而且只有横切磁场的那一部分运动才算数。这门课里就两种情况通吃全场：速度垂直于磁场，和速度平行于磁场。
cnpop.2: 场强 B 的单位是特斯拉（T）。这条公式只在速度垂直于磁场时才好使。一旦电荷沿着磁场——或者反着磁场——方向跑，受力直接归零。一个质子顺着磁感线射出去，那就是匀速直线一条道走到黑。
cnpop.3: Trap 不横切，没有力，记牢这条铁律。一个沿着磁场方向运动的电荷，受到的磁力就是零——跑得再快、场再强都白搭。同样的道理也套在电流顺着磁场走的导线上（参见 Force on a current-carrying wire）。不先瞅一眼 v 和 B 之间的夹角，张口就上 F = qvB，这是经典翻车现场。
cnpop.4: 力的方向，靠右手拍掌定则一掌拍出来，如 FIG 4.1 所示。右手摊平，大拇指跟四指成直角：大拇指顺着正电荷的速度，四指顺着磁场——这时候手掌面朝的方向就是力的方向。这个力永远同时垂直于速度和磁场，雷打不动。
cnpop.5: 碰到负电荷？把结果反过来就完事了。要么照常拍一掌再把力翻个面，要么干脆让大拇指逆着电子的运动指：一个往左跑的负电荷，等价于一个往右跑的正电荷。这个反转操作，是每道电子束题目的标配套路。
cnpop.6: 因为 v、B 和 F 三者两两垂直，在图里总有一个会沿着视线方向、正对着你怼过来。指进纸面的磁场画成一格格 × 符号，指出纸面的画成 • 符号；这套约定在 Into and out of the page 里讲得明明白白。
cnpop.7: Worked example. Lucia 盯着一束质子在研究，每个质子以 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹ 的速度、垂直于一个 0.25 T 的匀强磁场飞奔。磁场对单个质子施加的力的大小是 F = qvB = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 4.0 × 10⁶ × 0.25 = 1.6 × 10⁻¹³ N，方向按拍掌定则垂直于速度。
cnpop.8: 一个大小恒定、又永远垂直于速度的力——这不就是匀速圆周运动的标准开局嘛，Unit 3 AOS 1 里早见过了。所以电荷横切匀强磁场，乖乖画起圆来：磁力华丽变身向心力。把 qvB 和 mv²/r 划上等号一捣鼓，半径就出来了。
cnpop.9: 顺手就能拽出四条结论。跑得越快的粒子，圈划得越大（r ∝ v）；场越强，圈拐得越紧（r ∝ 1/B）。越重的粒子惯性越大，转弯的圈也越大（r ∝ m）。把电荷的正负号一翻，力跟着翻面，所以同样速度冲进同一磁场的电子和质子，绕圈方向正好相反。
cnpop.10: 磁力对电荷不做功，一分钱都不出。做功得有一个沿着运动方向的力分量，可磁力压根没有这玩意儿：它在每一瞬间都死死垂直于速度。速率恒定，动能恒定，变的只有运动方向，如 FIG 4.2 所示。磁场能给带电粒子掌舵转向，却动不了它的速率半分——想改速率，那得请 electric field 出马。
cnpop.11: Key idea 匀强磁场只改运动电荷的方向，从不碰它的速率。速率、动能、半径全都纹丝不动，速度矢量只是在原地转头。
cnpop.12: Worked example. Anika 把一个电子以 2.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ 的速度、垂直射进一个 1.5 mT 的匀强磁场。相对论效应先忽略不计。第一步先把场强换算了：1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T。然后 r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 2.0 × 10⁷) ÷ (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 1.5 × 10⁻³) = 7.6 × 10⁻² m；这电子就以一个永不改变的速率，绕着 7.6 cm 的半径打转。
cnpop.13: Trap 毫特斯拉警告。场强经常用 mT 报数：代入前先换算，比如 2.5 mT = 2.5 × 10⁻³ T。直接把原始数字怼进去，力会算大 1000 倍、半径算小 1000 倍，最坑的是这个错答案看起来还人模人样、特别像那么回事。
cnpop.14: Connects 澳大利亚同步加速器的储存环，就是 r = mv/qB 放大到一整栋楼的尺寸：偏转磁铁把电子摁在一条闭合圆形轨道上。转弯就是加速，而 Unit 4 会告诉你加速的电荷会辐射——这恰恰就是这个环能当光源用的原因。整套加速器流水线会在 8 · Motors & accelerators 里组装齐活。
cnpop.15: 电流说白了就是电荷在动，所以一根横切磁场、载着电流的导线必然挨力：磁场作用在每一个漂移的电荷上，而这些电荷又跑不出金属，只好把力一股脑甩给整根导线。对一根垂直于匀强磁场的直导体来说：
cnpop.16: n 数的是有多少根导体并排泡在磁场里：一个 20 匝的线圈，n = 20，受力就是单根导线的 20 倍。只有真正待在场内的那段长度才往 l 里算（注意是小写 l，跟公式表上一模一样）。
cnpop.17: 方向还是拍掌定则说了算，只不过这回大拇指改指惯例电流方向：大拇指顺 I，四指顺 B，手掌面朝 F，如 FIG 4.3 所示。那条零力规则在这儿原汁原味地回归：电流要是平行或反平行于磁场，受力为零。一个平躺在磁场里的矩形线圈，电流横切磁场的那两条边挨力，电流顺着磁场跑的那两条边一点力都没有。就这一条事实，正是下一节直流电动机的命门所在。
cnpop.18: Worked example. Magnus 在测一个 20 匝的扁平线圈。线圈的一条边长 0.050 m，载着 1.5 A 的电流，垂直于一个 0.30 T 的匀强磁场。磁场对这条边施加的力的大小是 F = nIlB = 20 × 1.5 × 0.050 × 0.30 = 0.45 N。对面那条边载着同样大小、方向相反的电流，所以它挨到一个大小相等、方向相反的力——这一对力，正好能把线圈转起来。
cnpop.19: Connects 这一节就是电动机的原理本体：磁场让载流线圈动起来。Unit 3 AOS 3 把它倒着放——让线圈在磁场里运动反过来驱动出电流——而这个反转，也就是靠磁通量变化实现的电磁感应，正是电网里每一台发电机的看家本领。
cnpop.20: 磁力问题天生是三维的：v（或者 I）、B 和 F 两两垂直，所以这仨绝不可能同时乖乖躺在纸面上。标准约定是让其中一个沿视线方向、画成一支箭：从箭尾看过去——交叉的箭羽 ×，指进纸面；迎着箭头看过来——尖尖的箭头 •，指出纸面。每一张用到这些符号的图都该配个图例，比如“× = field into the page”。
cnpop.21: 任何方向题都有一套稳如老狗的流程：第一步，从符号上读出磁场方向。第二步，把摊平的右手大拇指顺着正电荷的速度指——碰上电子就反着指——或者顺着惯例电流指。第三步，让四指对齐磁场；这时候手掌面朝的方向就是力的方向。
cnpop.22: 举个栗子，一个电子沿纸面向上运动，穿过一个指出纸面（•）的磁场。对一个向上运动的正电荷，拍掌定则给出的力指向右；可电子是负电荷，把它一反转，于是磁场对这个电子施加的力指向纸面的左边。

ai.0: Here’s something a little unsettling about magnetism: a magnetic field can shove a moving charge around, yet leave a stationary one completely untouched — and even for a moving charge, it only grabs the part of the motion that cuts across the field. In this topic you’ll meet the force law for lone charges and for currents, watch those forces curl particles into circles, and pick up the neat notation physicists use to draw all of this in three dimensions.
ai.1: Sit a charge still in a magnetic field and nothing happens — no force at all. The charge has to be moving, and even then only the slice of its motion that crosses the field counts for anything. Happily, just two cases cover everything you’ll meet in this course: the velocity pointing straight across the field, and the velocity running straight along it.
ai.2: We measure the field strength B in tesla (T). The formula does its job when the velocity is perpendicular to the field. Line the charge up parallel — or antiparallel — to the field instead, and the force vanishes to exactly zero. Fire a proton straight along a field line and it just sails on in a straight line at constant speed, as if the field weren’t there.
ai.3: Trap No crossing, no force. A charge gliding along the field direction feels zero magnetic force — no matter how fast it goes, no matter how mighty the field. The same goes for a wire whose current flows along the field (see Force on a current-carrying wire). Reaching for F = qvB before you’ve checked the angle between v and B is one of the easiest mistakes to make.
ai.4: To find which way the force points, use the right-hand slap rule, drawn in FIG 4.1. Hold your right hand flat with the thumb cocked at right angles to the fingers: thumb along the velocity of a positive charge, fingers along the field — and your palm now pushes in the direction of the force. That force always comes out perpendicular to both the velocity and the field.
ai.5: Got a negative charge? Just flip the answer. You can either run the slap rule and reverse the force, or aim your thumb against the electron’s motion — a negative charge heading left behaves exactly like a positive charge heading right. That little reversal turns up in every electron-beam problem you’ll ever solve.
ai.6: Since v, B and F are all mutually perpendicular, one of the three almost always ends up pointing straight at you (or away) in a diagram. A field heading into the page gets drawn as a grid of × symbols, and a field coming out of the page as • symbols; the full convention is laid out in Into and out of the page.
ai.7: Worked example. Lucia is studying a beam in which every proton races along at 4.0 × 10⁶ m s⁻¹, square-on to a uniform 0.25 T magnetic field. The size of the force the field lands on one proton is F = qvB = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 4.0 × 10⁶ × 0.25 = 1.6 × 10⁻¹³ N, pointing perpendicular to the velocity just as the slap rule promises.
ai.8: A force that never changes size and always stays perpendicular to the velocity — that’s precisely the recipe for uniform circular motion you met back in Unit 3 AOS 1. So a charge crossing a uniform magnetic field traces out a circle, with the magnetic force playing the role of the centripetal force. Set qvB equal to mv²/r, rearrange, and out drops the radius.
ai.9: Four nice consequences fall straight out of that. A faster particle carves a wider circle (r ∝ v), while a stronger field whips it into a tighter one (r ∝ 1/B). A heavier particle, dragging more inertia, swings around on a wider circle too (r ∝ m). And flip the sign of the charge and you flip the force — so electrons and protons entering the same field with the same velocity wind around in opposite directions.
ai.10: Here’s the kicker: the magnetic force does no work on the charge at all. Work needs a force component pointing along the motion, and the magnetic force never has one — it sits perpendicular to the velocity at every single instant. The speed holds steady, the kinetic energy holds steady, and only the direction of travel shifts, as shown in FIG 4.2. A magnetic field can steer a charged particle beautifully, but it can never speed it up or slow it down — any change in speed has to come from an electric field.
ai.11: Key idea A uniform magnetic field turns a moving charge’s direction but never touches its speed. Speed, kinetic energy and radius all stay put; only the velocity swings around.
ai.12: Worked example. Anika launches an electron at 2.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ into a uniform 1.5 mT field, dead perpendicular to the field. Ignore relativistic effects. Convert the field first: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ × 2.0 × 10⁷) ÷ (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 1.5 × 10⁻³) = 7.6 × 10⁻² m; the electron loops around with a radius of 7.6 cm, at a speed that never budges.
ai.13: Trap Millitesla. Field strengths get quoted in mT all the time, so convert before you substitute — as in 2.5 mT = 2.5 × 10⁻³ T. Drop the raw number straight in and you’ll make the force 1000 times too big and the radius 1000 times too small, and the worst part is the wrong answer will look perfectly believable.
ai.14: Connects The Australian Synchrotron’s storage ring is just r = mv/qB blown up to the size of a building: bending magnets pin electrons onto a closed circular path. Turning means accelerating, and Unit 4 will reveal that accelerating charges radiate — which is exactly why the ring shines as a light source. The whole accelerator pipeline gets pieced together in 8 · Motors & accelerators.
ai.15: A current is nothing but charge on the move, so a wire carrying current across a magnetic field feels a force too: the field pushes on every drifting charge, and since those charges can’t escape the metal, they hand the push straight on to the wire itself. For a straight conductor sitting at right angles to a uniform field:
ai.16: n simply counts how many conductors lie side by side in the field: for a coil of 20 turns, n = 20 and the force comes out 20 times the force on a single wire. Only the length actually inside the field pulls its weight in l (and watch the lowercase l, exactly as it appears on the formula sheet).
ai.17: The slap rule hands you the direction again, except now your thumb points along the conventional current: thumb along I, fingers along B, palm facing along F, as shown in FIG 4.3. And the zero-force case comes roaring back in full: if the current runs parallel or antiparallel to the field, the force is zero. Lay a rectangular coil in a field and the two sides whose current crosses the field feel forces, while the two sides whose current runs along the field feel nothing. Hold onto that one fact — it’s the whole secret behind the DC motor coming up in the next topic.
ai.18: Worked example. Magnus is testing a flat coil of 20 turns. One side of the coil is 0.050 m long, carries a current of 1.5 A and lies at right angles to a uniform 0.30 T field. The size of the force the field exerts on that side is F = nIlB = 20 × 1.5 × 0.050 × 0.30 = 0.45 N. The opposite side carries the same current the other way, so it cops an equal force in the opposite direction — a pair of forces that can spin the coil around.
ai.19: Connects This is the motor principle in a nutshell: a magnetic field sets a current-carrying coil moving. Unit 3 AOS 3 runs the whole thing backwards — drag a coil through a field and you drive a current — and that reversal, electromagnetic induction via changing magnetic flux, is how every generator on the grid does its job.
ai.20: Magnetic-force problems live in three dimensions: v (or I), B and F are mutually perpendicular, so there’s no way to fit all three flat on the page at once. The clever fix is to send one of them along your line of sight and picture it as an arrow: look at it from behind and you see the crossed fletching of the tail — ×, into the page; watch it fly toward you and you see the point — •, out of the page. Any diagram using these symbols should come with a key, something like "× = field into the page".
ai.21: Here’s a dependable routine for any direction question. First, read the field direction off the symbols. Second, lay your flat right hand’s thumb along the velocity of positive charge — reversed for an electron — or along the conventional current. Third, swing your fingers around to line up with the field; your palm is now facing along the force.
ai.22: Take an example: an electron travels up the page through a field pointing out of the page (•). For a positive charge moving up the page, the slap rule throws the force to the right — but the electron’s negative charge flips that, so the force the field exerts on the electron points to the left of the page.
